text: During a robotic prostatectomy, the surgeon performs a modified apical dissection by carefully removing fat from the apex. Superficial dorsal veins are cauterized to maintain hemostasis, exposing the endopelvic fascia and revealing the location of the puboprostatic ligaments. The detached fat pad is then removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia, separating the prostate from the pelvic side wall. The left endopelvic fascia is incised, revealing a superficial vein on the prostate capsule, which is controlled with a bipolar instrument due to its rare anatomical occurrence. The tabernus nerve is visible running posterior and lateral to the prostate, avoiding risk of injury. A perforating vessel between the sidewall and prostate, lateral to the puboprostatic ligament, is controlled with bipolar before division.

The surgeon places a stitch over the apex of the prostate to preserve apical structures responsible for urinary continence. Instruments are used to squeeze the prostate away, isolating the dorsal vein complex to control the blood vessels within it.

The surgeon uses the left-sided instrument to squeeze the prostate away, isolating the dorsal vein structures. Cauterization is performed between the left hand and the previously placed stitch, ensuring the stitch is not cut to prevent significant bleeding and avoiding violation of the prostate tissue.

The surgeon isolates the urethra and divides it to expose the Foley catheter, ensuring the dissection is just beyond the prostate apex while preserving urethral tissue length. The Foley catheter is pulled back to reveal only its tip. The posterior urethral dissection follows the urethral stump's contour to avoid damaging the prostate apex. After detachment, the prostate is placed in an endocatch bag for removal.
